segments.title: Giulio Cesare as design, luck, and fleet status
segments.summary: Giulio Cesare is presented as a Conte di Cavour-class battleship whose design, modernization, and survival made her a useful measure of Italian naval status.
segments.body: The lecture uses Giulio Cesare to explain how the Regia Marina sought advantage through broadside weight, efficient centerline layout, visual presence, and later reconstruction. Dr. Clarke then follows the ship through wartime episodes including Calabria, Taranto, convoy operations, surrender, and Soviet service, treating her repeated escapes as part of the episode's "lucky" framing. The closing counterfactual connects her sister Leonardo da Vinci to treaty-era force levels and asks how one more viable Italian battleship might have changed Mediterranean planning.

evidence.note: The opening frames Giulio Cesare and the Conte di Cavour class as Italian answers to French battleship design, emphasizing 13-gun broadside weight, modernization potential, and the ship's charmed service life.
The design section lists the original arrangement, machinery, speed, range, armament, armor, and Mediterranean conning-tower logic.
The wartime and postwar section describes Taranto positioning, Cape Spartivento, Naples damage, Sirte, surrender, Soviet transfer, and the disputed 1955 loss.
The conclusion argues that the Conte di Cavour class helped secure Italy's perceived naval equality with France.
